title: Local Coherence: When Words Don't Listen to Context
subtitle: Self-organization and bottom-up interference in sentence processing

children.children: Imagine you're sitting in front of a screen showing four objects: a white cake, a brown cake, a white car, and a brown car. Someone says:
"The boy will eat the white...."
Quick — where do your eyes go? Obviously to the white cake. 'Eat' means food, 'white' narrows it to the white cake. Your brain is brilliant at combining context. Case closed.

Except it's not closed. Because researchers tracking eye movements found something strange: when you hear 'white,' your eyes ALSO glance at the white car. Not as much as the white cake — but reliably MORE than the brown car.
Think about that. 'Eat' should completely rule out cars. Nobody eats cars. There is zero contextual support for looking at a car. And yet — the white car gets more looks than the brown car. WHY?

This is LOCAL COHERENCE. The word 'white' activates 'white things' — ALL white things — regardless of what 'eat' has already established. The bottom-up signal ('white' → anything white) persists even when the top-down context ('eat' → only edible things) should suppress it completely.

props.title: The Visual World Experiment
props.prompt: Watch what listeners fixate at each word in the sentence 'The boy will eat the white cake.' Four objects are on screen: 🎂 white cake, 🍪 brown cake, 🚗 white car, 🟫 brown car.

scenarios.reveal: Eyes are roughly evenly distributed. No strong signal yet — 'the boy will' doesn't favor any object. All four get about equal looks.

ANTICIPATION kicks in. Eyes shift to the TWO cakes — both the white and brown cake get more fixations. The verb 'eat' activates the concept of food, and listeners start looking at edible things BEFORE hearing what the edible thing is. This is top-down context at work.

Here's where it gets interesting. The white CAKE gets the most looks — context and color converge. But the white CAR also gets a bump! More looks than the brown car, even though neither car is edible. The word 'white' has sent out a bottom-up signal to everything white in the scene, and context can't fully suppress it.

Now fixations converge on the white cake. The bottom-up interference from the white car fades. But it WAS there — measurably, reliably, in study after study. The word 'white' briefly activated its associations independently of context. It didn't fully listen to 'eat.'

children.children: This tiny effect — a few extra glances at the white car — reveals something profound about how language works in the brain. Words are not obedient servants of context. They have their own agendas. And sometimes, like a bird in a flock that briefly veers off course, they activate associations that the rest of the sentence didn't ask for.

props.title: Self-Organization: Structure From the Bottom Up

children.children: To understand why words don't fully obey context, we need a concept from complexity science: SELF-ORGANIZATION.

Watch a flock of starlings at dusk. Thousands of birds swirl in breathtaking patterns — swooping, contracting, expanding as one. It looks choreographed. But there's no choreographer. No lead bird. No plan.

Each bird follows simple local rules: stay close to your neighbors, match their speed, don't collide. From these tiny, LOCAL interactions, GLOBAL structure emerges. The murmuration — that stunning aerial dance — is an emergent property. Nobody designed it. It self-organized.

props.concept: Language as a Flock of Words
props.analogy: Each word in a sentence is like a bird in a flock. It has its own momentum — its own associations, meanings, and connections built up over a lifetime of experience. When it enters the sentence, it interacts with its neighbors. Structure emerges from those interactions. But each word also retains some of its independent momentum. It doesn't FULLY surrender to the flock.
props.insight: The key word is SEMI-autonomous. Birds in a flock mostly follow the group — but they also have their own flight tendencies. Words in a sentence mostly follow context — but they also activate their own associations. 'White' mostly cooperates with 'eat' to point at food. But it also, independently, activates 'white things.' The flock mostly moves together. But individual birds sometimes veer.

children.children: This is Kukona, Cho, Magnuson, and Tabor's (2014) insight: sentence processing is a SELF-ORGANIZING system. Meaning isn't dictated top-down by context. It's not assembled bottom-up by words alone. It EMERGES from the interaction of semi-autonomous elements — each word contributing its associations while also being shaped by its neighbors.

props.analogy: Each word is like a person at a meeting. They arrive with their own opinions (bottom-up associations). As the meeting progresses, a group consensus forms (contextual meaning). Most people go along with the consensus most of the time. But sometimes someone says something that reflects their own opinion rather than the group's direction. That's not a failure of the meeting — it's a natural property of a system made of semi-autonomous agents.
props.insight: The white car fixation is like someone at the 'eating' meeting briefly mentioning cars — because the topic of 'white things' was raised and they couldn't help but think of white cars too. The meeting corrects course quickly. But the momentary digression reveals that the participants aren't just puppets of the agenda.

children.children: Here's the deep question: WHY would the brain build a system where words don't fully obey context? Wouldn't it be better to have perfect top-down control — hear 'eat,' immediately suppress all non-food?
Kukona et al.'s neural network model reveals the answer, and it's elegant: LEARNING ORDER MATTERS.
Think about how a child — or a neural network — learns language. What must you learn FIRST?

First-order associations must be learned BEFORE higher-order associations. You must learn 'white → white things' before you can learn 'eat + white → edible white things.' You must learn what words MEAN before you can learn how context MODIFIES those meanings.

This is a logical necessity. You can't learn that 'eat' restricts 'white' to edible-white-things until you've already learned what 'white' means on its own. The first-order association (white → white things) is the FOUNDATION. The higher-order association (eat + white → edible white things) is built ON TOP of that foundation.
And here's the crucial insight: you can't delete the foundation without collapsing the building.

props.title: Learning Trajectory: From Bottom-Up to Context-Modulated
props.prompt: Watch how a neural network's response to 'white' changes over training. Early on, it activates everything white. Later, context suppresses irrelevant items — but never completely.

scenarios.reveal: The network activates ALL white things equally: white cake, white car, white snow, white paper, white clouds. It has learned first-order associations — 'white' connects to anything white. No context sensitivity yet. Every bird in the flock is flying independently.

Context begins to help. 'Eat' partially suppresses non-food items. White cake: HIGH activation. White car: REDUCED but not zero. The network is learning higher-order associations — 'eat + white = edible white things' — but the first-order association (white → all white things) still bleeds through. The flock is forming, but individual birds still veer.

Context is now powerful. White cake: VERY HIGH. White car: LOW — but still above the brown car! The suppression is strong but never 100% complete. The first-order association persists as a residual signal. Like a bird in a tight flock that still wobbles slightly on its own trajectory.

scenarios.input: Why can't training eliminate the interference completely?
scenarios.reveal: Because the higher-order associations (context-dependent meanings) are BUILT ON the first-order associations (context-free meanings). Eliminating 'white → white things' would also damage 'eat + white → edible white things' — because the latter DEPENDS on the former. You can't remove the ground floor without collapsing the upper floors. Bottom-up interference isn't a bug. It's a RESIDUE of the learning process — a developmental necessity that never fully goes away.

props.title: The Foundation Problem: Why First-Order Associations Can't Be Erased

children.children: This is a deep point about neural network learning that extends far beyond language. In ANY hierarchical learning system, lower-order representations are the substrate on which higher-order representations are built.

Consider: a network that learned 'white → white things' and then perfectly suppressed that association in the context of 'eat' would have DISCONNECTED 'white' from 'white things.' But it still needs that connection! In a new sentence — 'Paint the wall white' — the network needs 'white' to activate the color white, not edible things.

The first-order association must remain ACTIVE and ACCESSIBLE because it's used by many different higher-order associations. 'Eat white,' 'paint white,' 'wear white,' 'the white house' — all of these depend on 'white → white things' as their foundation. Suppressing it perfectly in one context would impair it in all others.

This is why the interference diminishes with training but never fully disappears. More training strengthens the higher-order contextual override — but it can never eliminate the first-order association without catastrophic side effects. The foundation is permanent. The building gets taller and more sophisticated, but the ground floor is always there.

props.title: Anticipation AND Interference: Not a Contradiction

children.children: Here's something that puzzled researchers for years. Two seemingly contradictory findings coexist in the same experiments:
Finding 1 — ANTICIPATION: When listeners hear 'eat,' they look at the cake BEFORE hearing 'cake.' Context drives rapid, predictive eye movements toward relevant objects. The system is smart!
Finding 2 — INTERFERENCE: When listeners hear 'white,' they also look at the white car — an irrelevant object. Bottom-up associations leak through despite context. The system is dumb!
Smart AND dumb? At the same time? How?

The self-organization framework resolves this beautifully. In a flock of birds, two things are simultaneously true: (1) the flock has a clear overall direction — a GROUP trajectory that emerges from coordination, and (2) individual birds wobble and deviate from that trajectory. The group is organized. The individuals are semi-autonomous.

Anticipation IS the group trajectory. When 'eat' activates the concept of food, the whole system — the whole flock — tilts toward edible things. Eyes move to cakes. That's the emergent, organized, top-down structure working.

Interference IS the individual wobble. When 'white' activates white things, that includes the white car — because 'white' is a semi-autonomous agent with its own associations. The flock is headed toward food, but this particular bird also has a connection to cars.

props.title: Speed vs. Accuracy: The Fundamental Trade-off

children.children: These aren't bugs — they're features of a system optimized for SPEED and FLEXIBILITY. Consider the alternative: a system with perfect top-down control, where 'eat' completely suppresses all non-food associations.

Such a system would need to fully resolve context BEFORE activating word meanings — which would be SLOW. You'd have to wait for the system to check context before each word could contribute its meaning. In a conversation happening at 3-4 words per second, that delay would be catastrophic.

Instead, the brain uses a fast-and-loose strategy: let every word activate its associations immediately (fast!), while SIMULTANEOUSLY letting context shape the overall pattern (smart!). The cost is occasional interference — the white car gets a glance. The benefit is speed — you understand 'eat the white cake' in milliseconds, with anticipation already pointing you to the right answer before the sentence is even finished.

If you've read our Sausage Machine tutorial, the two-stage architecture of human parsing should be familiar: a local, shortsighted Preliminary Phrase Packager (PPP) feeds into a global, structure-building Sentence Structure Supervisor (SSS). Kukona et al.'s self-organization framework maps onto this architecture — and onto transformers — with striking precision.

props.title: Mapping Self-Organization to Transformer Layers
props.prompt: See how the self-organization framework from Kukona et al. maps onto what we've discovered about GPT-2's layer structure.

scenarios.reveal: In the early and middle layers, each token activates its associations REGARDLESS of context. 'White' activates white things. 'Eat' activates eating-related concepts. These are first-order associations — the foundation. Like birds that haven't yet coordinated into a flock, each word is flying on its own momentum. This is the bottom-up processing that Kukona et al. showed must come first in learning.

In the later layers, context kicks in. 'Eat + white' gets resolved to 'edible white things.' The flock forms. Higher-order patterns override (but don't eliminate) local associations. The SSS takes the PPP's raw packages and integrates them into a coherent whole. This is where anticipation lives — the system has enough context to predict what's coming next.

Layer 11 acts as a consensus detector — monitoring whether the bottom-up signals from earlier layers fit the top-down contextual structure. When they do: smooth processing, high consensus. When they don't: the exception handler fires. This is where the system notices that the bottom-up activation of 'white car' doesn't fit the 'eating' context.

In our L11 framework, Mode 2 is the 'exception handler without consensus breakdown.' The bottom-up signal arrives (white → white car), L11 notices it doesn't fit the global parse (we're talking about eating), but the STRUCTURE itself is fine — the sentence isn't ambiguous or broken. So consensus stays intact. The exception is noted but doesn't cause a crisis. This is exactly what happens with the white car: you notice it, but it doesn't derail comprehension.

scenarios.input: Why DO-SC Garden Paths Show Mode 2
scenarios.reveal: Consider 'The boy knew the answer was wrong.' At 'the answer,' the local word associations (verb + NP) look like a direct object — 'knew the answer.' But it's actually a complement clause — 'knew [that] the answer was wrong.' The LOCAL coherence (verb + NP = direct object) conflicts with the GLOBAL parse (it's a complement clause). L11 detects the mismatch (Mode 2) but the structure doesn't collapse — because the sentence is still perfectly grammatical. The flock wobbles but doesn't break apart.

children.children: The parallel is deep: Kukona et al. showed that first-order associations must be learned BEFORE higher-order ones. In transformers, early layers (first-order, local) process BEFORE late layers (higher-order, contextual). The same learning constraint that creates bottom-up interference in humans is ARCHITECTURALLY ENCODED in the transformer's layer structure. The flock metaphor scales: early layers are individual birds; late layers are the emergent murmuration.
The white car effect didn't come out of nowhere. It connects to one of the most famous findings in psycholinguistics, dating back to 1979.
David Swinney presented people with sentences like:
"Rumor had it that, for years, the government building had been plagued with problems. The man was not surprised when he found several spiders, roaches, and other BUGS in the corner of his room."

The context SCREAMS 'insects.' There is no ambiguity for a conscious reader. And yet — immediately after hearing 'bugs,' participants were FASTER to recognize the word SPY (related to surveillance bugs) than an unrelated control word.

ALL meanings of an ambiguous word are activated initially, even in overwhelmingly strong context. 'Bugs' activated insects AND surveillance devices AND software errors — everything — before context could suppress the irrelevant meanings.

props.title: Exhaustive Access: The Rule, Not the Exception

children.children: Swinney's finding has been replicated hundreds of times across languages and methods. It's called EXHAUSTIVE ACCESS — all meanings are accessed initially, and context selects among them afterward. The bottom-up signal (word → all meanings) arrives FIRST. The top-down signal (context → correct meaning) arrives SECOND.

Sound familiar? It's the same pattern as the white car. Bottom-up activation (white → all white things) arrives first. Top-down suppression (eat → only edible things) arrives second. The interference window is brief — maybe 200 milliseconds — but it's REAL.

This connects directly to our sense-stack work on polysemy resolution. In GPT-2, early layers activate ALL senses of an ambiguous word — just like Swinney's exhaustive access. 'Bank' in 'river bank' initially activates both the riverside AND the financial institution. Layer by layer, context suppresses the wrong sense. By L11, the system monitors whether the selected sense fits the global context.

The flock metaphor holds perfectly: each word-bird enters the flock carrying ALL its meanings. The flock's overall direction (context) gradually aligns the word toward the correct meaning. But the other meanings were there — briefly, measurably, consequentially. Individual birds always carry their own momentum into the flock.

props.title: From Swinney to Sense-Stacks: 45 Years of the Same Insight

children.children: The thread connecting Swinney (1979), Kukona et al. (2014), and our transformer layer analysis is remarkably consistent: BOTTOM-UP FIRST, TOP-DOWN SECOND.

Swinney showed it with lexical priming: all word senses activate before context selects. Kukona et al. showed it with eye tracking: word-feature associations activate before context constrains. Our sense-stack work shows it in transformers: early layers activate all senses before later layers resolve polysemy.

And Kukona et al.'s neural network explains WHY this must be so: first-order associations are the necessary foundation for higher-order contextual processing. You can't skip the bottom-up step. The flock can't form if the birds don't first take flight individually.

questions.question: In the 'eat the white...' experiment, why do listeners look at the white car more than the brown car?
options: They think the boy might eat a car
The word 'white' activates all white objects, including the car, regardless of the 'eat' context
They are confused by the sentence
The white car is more visually salient
questions.explanation: This is local coherence / bottom-up interference. The word 'white' activates its first-order associations (white → white things) semi-autonomously, even though the context ('eat') should restrict attention to edible things. The bottom-up signal persists despite top-down context — like a bird in a flock that briefly veers on its own trajectory.

questions.question: Why can't the brain (or a neural network) fully suppress bottom-up interference with more training?
options: The brain doesn't have enough neurons
First-order associations are the foundation that higher-order associations are built on — eliminating them would collapse the whole system
Context isn't strong enough
The experiments aren't long enough to see full suppression
questions.explanation: Higher-order associations ('eat + white → edible white things') depend on first-order associations ('white → white things') as their foundation. Erasing the first-order link would also destroy the higher-order one — and all other contexts that use 'white → white things' (like 'paint white,' 'wear white,' etc.). You can't remove the ground floor without collapsing the building.

questions.question: How does self-organization resolve the apparent contradiction between anticipation (smart context use) and interference (dumb bottom-up activation)?
options: They happen in different brain regions

Anticipation is the flock's overall direction (emergent group structure), while interference is individual elements retaining their own momentum — both are natural properties of a self-organizing system
Anticipation is real but interference is just noise
They happen at different times and never overlap
questions.explanation: In a self-organizing system, global structure (the flock's direction = anticipation) and local autonomy (individual birds veering = interference) coexist naturally. The system is simultaneously organized at the group level AND semi-autonomous at the individual level. This isn't a contradiction — it's the defining feature of self-organization.

questions.question: What did Swinney (1979) discover about the word 'bugs' in the context of 'spiders, roaches, and other bugs'?
options: Only the 'insects' meaning was activated
The word was processed more slowly due to ambiguity
ALL meanings (insects, surveillance, software) were initially activated, even though context strongly favored 'insects'
Participants couldn't determine the meaning without more context
questions.explanation: Swinney demonstrated exhaustive access: all meanings of an ambiguous word are activated initially, regardless of context. 'Bugs' primed SPY (surveillance meaning) even in an overwhelming insect context. Context suppresses the wrong meaning, but not instantly — the bottom-up activation of all senses comes FIRST, top-down selection comes SECOND.

questions.question: How does the self-organization framework map onto transformer layer structure?
options: It doesn't — transformers work completely differently from the brain

Early layers (0–6) do context-independent, first-order processing; late layers (7–11) do context-dependent integration — mirroring the learning-order constraint that first-order associations precede higher-order ones
All layers process context equally
Only the final layer matters for meaning
questions.explanation: The learning-order constraint (first-order before higher-order) is architecturally encoded in transformers: early layers activate associations locally (like first-order associations), while late layers integrate context globally (like higher-order associations). Layer 11 monitors consensus — detecting when bottom-up signals from early layers conflict with the top-down contextual structure. The same computational logic, different substrate.

children.children: • Kukona, A., Cho, P.W., Magnuson, J.S., & Tabor, W. (2014). 'Lexical interference effects in sentence processing: Evidence from the visual world paradigm and self-organizing models.' Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition.
• Swinney, D.A. (1979). 'Lexical access during sentence comprehension: (Re)consideration of context effects.' Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior, 18, 645–659.
• Tabor, W., & Hutchins, S. (2004). 'Evidence for self-organized sentence processing: Digging-in effects.' Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition.

• Tanenhaus, M.K., Spivey-Knowlton, M.J., Eberhard, K.M., & Sedivy, J.C. (1995). 'Integration of visual and linguistic information in spoken language comprehension.' Science, 268, 1632–1634. — The visual world paradigm that made these experiments possible.
